statement.markdown: You are given an array of integers. Find and print the Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS). A subsequence is a sequence that can be derived from another sequence by deleting some or no elements without changing the order of the remaining elements. An increasing subsequence is one in which every element is greater than its previous element.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer n, the length of the array. The second line contains n space-separated integers representing the array elements.
statement.outputFormat: The length of the LIS followed by the LIS itself, space-separated.

examples.explanation: The longest increasing subsequence is the entire array.
The longest increasing subsequence is just 5.

hints.text: The problem asks for the Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS), which is a subsequence where every element is greater than its previous one. Think about how you can build up this subsequence iteratively.
Consider using dynamic programming to solve this problem. You can create an array where each element represents the length of the LIS ending at that index. Think about how you can fill up this array.

To fill up the dynamic programming array, for each element in the input array, compare it with all previous elements. If the current element is greater than a previous element, it can potentially extend the LIS ending at the previous element. Think about how you can keep track of the actual subsequences.

editorial.title: Printing the Longest Increasing Subsequence
editorial.intuition: The problem requires finding the longest increasing subsequence (LIS) in a given array and printing it. A key observation is that the LIS ending at index i can be constructed by extending the LIS ending at a previous index j if the element at index i is greater than the element at index j.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve generating all possible subsequences and checking if they are increasing. This would result in an exponential time complexity due to the large number of subsequences. The given reference solution uses dynamic programming to improve the time complexity to O(n^2).
editorial.betterSolution: The dynamic programming approach uses two arrays: dp to store the lengths of the LIS ending at each index, and prev to store the previous index in the LIS. This allows for efficient construction of the LIS.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses a single pass through the array, maintaining the dp and prev arrays. It then finds the index of the maximum length LIS and prints it, followed by the sequence itself.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the dynamic programming approach can be shown by induction. The base case is when i = 0, where dp[0] = 1 and prev[0] = -1. For i > 0, if nums[i] > nums[j] and dp[i] < dp[j] + 1, then dp[i] and prev[i] are updated correctly. The LIS is then correctly constructed by backtracking from the index of the maximum length LIS.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run with a sample input, such as [10, 22, 9, 33, 21, 50, 41, 60, 80], helps verify the correctness of the approach. The output should be the length of the LIS and the sequence itself.
commonMistakes: Not initializing the dp and prev arrays correctly
Not updating the dp and prev arrays correctly when a longer LIS is found
Not handling the base case correctly
Not printing the sequence in the correct order
Not finding the index of the maximum length LIS correctly
Not handling edge cases such as an empty input array
